History of to-morrow

However opinions may differ, there is one fact which no one
will feel inclined to dispute - namely that our world, in ~~its~~ {is} {an}
abnormal moral condition.
~~abnormal state to-day, is facing a severe moral crisis.~~ I believe {in}
particular ~~when~~ you in America look across to Europe, you must
have the feeling that all the peoples and nations are victims of a
morbid irritability. The slightest pretext is sufficient to evoke
boundless agitation. Races, social classes and states, no less than
individuals, seem more inclined to hate one another than to live on
good terms. Neither the individual nor the nation has faith in the
possibility of tranquil and productive development. On the contrary,
all of us ~~who live in Europe~~ - and I believe that you will not contra-
dict me when I say this - are living under ~~the constant impression~~,
the constant apprehension, that a violent explosion may take place
any second.

To what is this state of tension due? I believe that this
fever, ~~this stagnation in the normal circulation of our blood which~~ {in}
~~dates from the War, is caused by the same old bacillus.~~ {due to a} {fostered by the war.} We must
remember that the War years accustomed the people in every country to
a violent heightening of emotion. Wars cannot be waged in cold blood
and with a cool mind. A tremendous injection of passion was necess-
ary to enable ~~an~~ to endure a world war for {the} {terrible} {period} {of}
~~the~~ {the} {belligerent} {nations} {such} {an} {appalling}
four years. {Every day} ~~of those fifteen hundred days~~ {the} {instincts} {of} {hatred,} {anger,} {and} {embitterment} {had} {to}
{given} {fresh} {stimulus} ~~af~~ {aa} {in} {normal} {times}
be stimulated ~~incessantly~~ in all the warring states. Thus millions
of human beings who were ~~really indifferent and~~ entirely peaceable,
three or four hundred millions of them - please try to visualise this
figure - became accustomed {through} {fifteen} {hundred} {days} - ~~for four endless years to producing and~~ 
{generating} ~~within themselves more hatred and enmity than was natural~~ {every day of those fifteen hundred days}
pages.transcription_b:
History of - to - morrow

However opinions may differ, there is one fact which no one
will feel inclined to dispute - namely that our world, in {is} ~~its~~ {an}
~~abnormal moral condition.~~
~~abnormal state to-day, is facing a severe moral crisis. I believe~~ {In}
particular ~~that~~ when you in America look across to Europe, you must
have the feeling that all the peoples and nations are victims of a
morbid irritability. The slightest pretext is sufficient to evoke
boundless agitation. Races, social classes and states, no less than
individuals, seem more inclined to hate one another than to live on
good terms. Neither the individual nor the nation has faith in the
possibility of tranquil and productive development. On the contrary,
all of us ~~who live in Europe~~ - and I believe that you will not contra-
dict me when I say this - are living under ~~the constant impression,~~ 
the constant apprehension, that a violent explosion may take place
any second.

To what is this state of tension due? I believe that this
fever, ~~this stagnation in the normal circulation of~~ {in} our blood ~~which~~ 
~~dates from the War,~~ is ~~caused by the same old~~ {due to a} bacillus {fostered by the war}. We must
remember that the War years accustomed the people in every country to
a violent heightening of emotion. Wars cannot be waged in cold blood
and with a cool mind. A tremendous injection of passion was necess-
ary to enable ~~us~~ {the belligerent nations} to endure a world war for ~~the terrible~~ {such an appalling} period ~~of~~ 
{every day} ~~of those fifteen hundred days~~ {of}
four years. The instincts of hatred, anger, and embitterment had to
{given fresh stimulus} ~~be stimulated incessantly~~ in all the warring states. Thus millions
{in normal times}
of human beings who were ~~really indifferent and~~ entirely peaceable,
three or four hundred millions of them - please try to visualise this
figure - became accustomed ~~for~~ {through fifteen hundred days -} four endless years ~~to producing and~~
{generating} ~~creating~~ within themselves more hatred and enmity than was natural
{every day of these fifteen hundred days}

{to become harmless. It was suddenly his duty to die peacefully in his bed and let everyone else do likewise. Such an abrupt transformation from years of brutality}

Those very things which ~~only~~ yesterday had been regarded as the highest
{human virtues - murder, plunder, hate -} reverted over night to the former
status of crimes. ~~Who for five years had been obliged to behave as a hero, was now~~
to them. Then came the Peace, and suddenly this obligation to hate,
to murder, to maintain one's passions at fever heat had to be turned
off at a word of command as one turns off a gas tap. {Violence, murder, had forbidden [?] [?] [?] [?]} ~~This~~ is con-
trary to Nature. When an organism has become accustomed to narcot-
ics or stimulants - to coffee, to nicotine, to morphia - it cannot
suddenly do without them. {And peace or no peace} The ~~need~~ {lust} to militarise oneself, to hate
and to fight, {once inflamed} has remained active in this generation. Only it has
changed its objective. ~~People no longer hate the same enemy of~~
~~their country as they did in 1914, but they continue to hate and~~
~~fight with the same dangerous passion.~~ {It has been turned inward. It has} changed to the hatred
of one system for another, of one party, one class, one race, one
ideology for another. ~~Essentially, however, the forms are the same~~
~~as they were in 1914, since they are conditioned by the need to form~~
~~groups and to act as groups in rivalry against other groups.~~ We are
{Only} said to be at peace, but our world is ~~dominated~~ {ruled} by a definitely
{bellicose} ~~warlike~~ mentality. {overexcite}

In what way can we eliminate this dangerous state of things?
How can we reduce the persistent feverish temperature? ~~How can we~~
~~humanise again the warlike atmosphere?~~ What antidote do we possess
for the ~~hatred~~ {venom} by which the organism is poisoned? ~~How can we over-~~
~~come the moral depression with which the atmosphere of our world is~~
~~charged?~~ For us {all of} {in all countries} this is the problem of problems, and I am of course
not so presumptuous as to think that I am the first to have discover-
ed it. Nor do I know of any complete solution. I am aware, and we
are all aware, how much has been attempted in this respect, and we
have to thank particularly American democracy and your Government for

pages.transcription_a:
- 3 -

having again and again emphasised the need for genuine peace and
permanent world understanding. Yet we have grown very distrustful of
conferences, proclamations and appeals. These may {perhaps} have
prevented or postponed trouble in individual cases, but they have not
{dispelled} {the} {atmosphere} {of} {suspicion} {and} {enmity}
altered the moral, or rather immoral, condition in which the world at
large finds itself to-day. It seems that the quiet, contemplative
voice of reason is too weak to be heard above the megaphone with which
organised propaganda is thundering its slogans throughout the world,
and it is in fact in the very nature of reason that its effects can
rarely appear immediately. Perhaps we shall even have to renounce
the attempt to cure the present generation, the War generation, which
has political control in most countries, ~~of its lauding of force, its~~
{apotheosis} {of} {war,} {and} {its} {mentality} {of} {hatred.} {perhaps} {our} {real}
{task}
work lies in the application of all our energies towards ensuring
{shall}
that at least the next generation, the youth of to-day, ~~should~~ not
succumb to this {same} infection, this {same} fever. Adults do not learn much, not
even through dire experiences, and all our efforts must be directed
towards reaching the young at an age when their souls are still like
plastic wax in the hands of their educators. If the new generation
is to be better, more humane, and above all happier than ours, into
whose lives the War cut deeply, striking us to the very core, it
must be better and more humanely educated. Within the framework of
this new education it seems to me that the most important element is
a different form and conception of history from those which were
imparted to us at school. Nothing so decisively shapes a young
person's political and individual attitude to life as the way in
which he has learned and comprehended history. {But} {all} {education} {is}
{based} {on} {example,} {and} {we} {can} {no} {better} {examples} {for} {the} {education}
{of} {mankind} {than} {those} {contained} {in} {the} {history} {of} {mankind.} {Only}
pages.transcription_b:
- 3 -

having again and again emphasised the need for genuine peace and
permanent world understanding. Yet we have grown very distrustful of
conferences, proclamations and appeals. {for years} These may {perhaps} have
prevented or postponed trouble in individual cases, but they have not
{dispelled the atmosphere of suspicion and enmity}
~~altered the moral, or rather immoral, condition~~ in which the world at
large finds itself to-day. It seems that the quiet, contemplative
voice of reason is too weak to be heard above the megaphone with which
organised propaganda is thundering its slogans throughout the world,
and it is in fact in the very nature of reason that its effects can
rarely appear immediately. Perhaps we shall even have to renounce
the attempt to cure the present generation, the War generation, which
has political control in most countries, ~~of its lauding of force, its~~
~~apotheosis of war, and its mentality of hatred.~~ {and} ~~Perhaps~~ our real
{task} ~~work~~ lies in the application of all our energies towards ensuring
that at least the next generation, the youth of to-day, ~~should~~ {shall} not
succumb to this {same} infection, this {same} fever. Adults do not learn much, not
even through dire experiences, and all our efforts must be directed
towards reaching the young at an age when their souls are still like
plastic wax in the hands of their educators. If the new generation
is to be better, more humane, and above all happier than ours, into
whose lives the War cut deeply, striking us to the very core, it
must be better and more humanely educated. Within the framework of
this new education it seems to me that the most important element is
a different form and conception of history from those which were
imparted to us at school. Nothing so decisively shapes a young
person's political and individual attitude to life as the way in
which he has learned and comprehended history. {But all education is based on example, and we can find no better examples for the education of mankind than those contained in the history of mankind. Only}

- 4 -
{To render this method effective, we must have a different form and conception of history from those which were imparted to us at school.} // How did we ourselves, especially those of us who live in
Europe, learn history at school? I must frankly confess that I myself
had forgotten. But a short while ago, while moving from one house to
another, I came upon an old text book from which I had learned history
at my high school in Austria. I should like to say, by the way, that
we make a mistake in throwing away our old schoolbooks too soon, for
there is nothing which enables us after a period of years to realise
so clearly the speed with which ideas and points of view change during
our lifetime. Well, there it was, my old, dog-eared history book, and
I found myself presented with the opportunity of examining once more
the form in which history had been taught to the present generation,
profoundly influencing its outlook. I began to read - and was truly
shocked. Good heavens! Was this the way in which the history of the
world had been presented to us when we were young, inexperienced, and
credulous? So inaccurately, so falsely, so tendentiously? I realised
at once something of which as a boy I had not had the faintest inkling,
namely that this was history specially dished up, coloured and dis-
guised, and that it had been done with a calculated and definite pur-
pose. ~~I realised,~~ {Since} the book had been printed in Austria and
officially approved for use in Austrian schools, {I realised} ~~that~~ it was deliberate-
ly intended to imbue ~~the youth of the nation~~ {us} with the conviction that
the Spirit directing the universe was working towards one ultimate
goal, namely, the greatness of Austria and the Austrian Empire. Yet
twelve hours by train - two hours by aeroplane to-day - would bring one
to France or to Italy, where history was served up to ~~our~~ {youngsters of my age} ~~youthful~~
contemporaries with an equally nationalistic bias. Gesta dei per
Francos - God, or the Spirit of History, worked only for France, for

- 5 -
{other}
the Motherland. Before we were able to look out clearly into the
world, we had spectacles put on our noses - tinted differently in
each country - so that from the very start we might look upon the
world not with the unrestricted vision of ~~free human beings~~ {as Weltbürger, citizens of the world}, but from
~~the viewpoint of national interest.~~ {as Staatsbürger, subjects of our state.} That which in the Germany of to-
day is called 'national education', that is to say, the early moulding
of mind and outlook to a uniform pattern, had already begun. {of national egoism} History,
which has no ~~other~~ {other moral} purpose than ~~the formation of a better mankind~~ ~~unless it is supremely objective,~~ was ladled out
to us with the sole purpose of turning us into ~~patriotic citizens,~~ {youngsters}
future soldiers, {and} docile subjects. {In the first place} Our humility towards the State and
its institutions was to be counterbalanced by the arrogance and dis-
trust with which we regarded all other peoples and races, {the latter to be} imbued as
~~we were with the doctrine~~ {Of all its manifold teachings and facts only one singled out was} that our Motherland was the best of all
Motherlands, our soldiers ~~had always been~~ the bravest of all soldiers, our generals the
most capable of all generals; that throughout the course of history
our nation had always been in the right, and that, whatever it might
do, it would continue to be in the right. My country right or wrong!

This was the first of the false paths upon which our steps
were set. And as I read through my old schoolbook from cover to
cover, it was not long before I realised what the second false path
had been. The book was so arranged - I am sorry that I have not
brought it with me, so that I might read certain passages to you - it
was so arranged that the most important historical events were accom-
panied by dates printed in the margin, like milestones showing the
distance mankind had travelled, and these dates we had to learn by
heart.

What were the events which were thus specially emphasised?

- 6 -

I looked through the book once more and established the fact that
ninety per cent of the dates were those of wars and battles. We had {to}
learn by heart the particular year B.C. when the battle of Salamis was
fought and the year of the battle of Cannae. We had to know how long
the First Punic War lasted and how long the Second, and so on through
the centuries, war after war, battle after battle, until Trafalgar,
Waterloo, and finally Sedan. {The great war, of course, did not yet figure in our books, but} We were ~~then~~ given the privilege of
learning ~~the~~ {its} dates ~~of the Great War~~ in a somewhat more personal and
tangible manner, ~~than was possible from books.~~ {later}

In this old primer there was little evidence that the three
thousand years {of our common history} ~~with which it dealt~~ had witnessed other developments,
thanks to which man had risen from a state of troglodyte cannibalism
to the heights of culture. Nor did it say much about the kings and
emperors, the statesmen and wise presidents, who quietly and unemotion-
ally had preserved peace for their countries and fostered progress.
It was only Hannibal, Scipio, Attila and Napoleon who were important.
Only men who had waged war were presented to us as heroes, and in this
way there was drummed into our young and receptive minds the constantly
repeated idea that the most important factor in this world was war and
the summit of achievement: ~~for an individual or a nation was~~ victory.
Our generation, ~~and it is I fear still the case with the younger~~ {more}
generation in all the countries of Europe, {today} was taught from the beginnin
that the decisive element in this world is success, that the employment
of force, including that of war, is not only permissible but desirable
if it is to the advantage of one's {own} country. ~~Well, we have all seen~~
~~the results of this teaching. They are evident in the apprehension,~~
~~hatred and unrest which to-day have thrown our world out of gear.~~

- 7 -

{But} The Great War destroyed many things. Among others it smashed
the ~~spectacles~~ {tinted} through which we young men were compelled to look, and
I can only repeat that I was truly shocked when I read my tattered old
schoolbook again with ~~different~~ {enlightened} eyes. For to what conclusion does
history lead us when it is related ~~exclusively as the history of wars?~~ {in such a one-sided manner? I say}
~~The conclusion to which we must inevitably come is profoundly pessim-~~
~~istic.~~ {a terribly} What does this eternal story of wars and victories ultimately
reveal? Something that is completely devoid of meaning, a mere tire-
some repetition! One army, one general, one nation defeats another;
strongholds surrender or do not surrender; countries become swollen by
annexations, only to lose their acquisitions again after a shorter or
longer interval. This eternal calendar of the wars of mankind ~~appears~~
~~to me no less tedious than would be a record of all the football matches~~ {is not only as senseless as}
that have been played throughout the world during the last fifty years, {would be}
~~but it inevitably leads us to a profoundly pessimistic of mankind. As long as~~
~~the victory going first to one side, then to the other. Are we to~~
~~learn from history nothing except that the nations have taken it in~~
~~turns to fight, despoil and enslave one another?~~ {but it inevitably leads us to a profoundly pessimistic view of mankind. As long as we lay the main stress on this descriptions of wars in our conception of history, we shall not get away from the depressing suspicion,}
~~That~~ humanity has ~~not~~
made ~~any~~ {no} real progress, but is still ~~wading about in a circle~~ {floundering about} in a ~~sea~~ {morass} of blood? {then go again} Or are we to ~~deduce~~ {to extoll as} progress ~~from~~ the fact that, whereas the
soldiers of Xerxes killed their opponents face to face with battleaxes,
those of Ludendorff were able to mow down ~~rows of men~~ {thousands} with machine-guns?
~~That we no longer pour boiling oil from the battlements of beseiged~~
~~castles, but hurl streams of fire through the nozzles of intricately~~
~~designed flame-throwers? That we obey the same old instincts, but en-~~
~~ploy improved mechanical devices;~~ that cannibalistic hordes have been
replaced by vast armies; and that the discordant warcries of savages
have been superseded by the braying of propaganda from loud-speaker

- 8 -
{better times,}
and radio? I must confess that as I read this old schoolbook of mine,
I found nothing in it that might have had any ~~elevating and~~ humanising
effect on young people. {nothing for our modern world} I found in it only the sad evidence of our
eternal reversion to the old barbarism. At last I was unable to
restrain my irritation and flung the book into a corner, for I realised
that this was the way in which my generation had been ~~tutored in prep-~~
~~aration for the~~ {drilled towards} ~~Great~~ War. It had helped to foster all the dangerous
and evil instincts which are poisoning the world to-day.

That was our education. That was how history was taught in
every country in Europe. The results are everywhere visible. It was
continually dinned into our ears, {that} ~~stamped upon our minds,~~ that victory
in war was the highest achievement of which a man or a nation was
capable, and that ~~it~~ was a matter of utter indifference what means were
employed to accomplish this end. No price was too high to pay - the
lives of ten thousand men, a hundred thousand, a million. And far
from the terrible experience of the Great War ~~having proved this unhum-~~
~~an, immoral conception to be criminal,~~ we see that it is being instill-
ed into the youth and adults of most European countries to-day with an
unprecedented intensity and exaggeration. Even in this country, across
thousands of miles of ocean, you can hear the raucous shouts of dictat-
ors demanding what they call an 'heroic' conception of life, preaching
that love of peace is a sign of debility and that a man can have no
greater ambition than to die for his country. We are witnessing to-day
a glorification of war ~~as the supreme purpose of existence, a glorific-~~
~~ation~~ such as not even the Spartans, not even the most barbaric of
ancient races, would have ventured to proclaim. We are witnessing, in
the name of national pride, a perversion of history which sets our
{blood aflame with loathing.}

9.

And more than this: not only is the past, not
only is history falsified, but even the immediate present.
During these last critical weeks I myself have experienced
it. One stood in front of the radio, turning the dial
from one station to another: Berlin, Paris, Rome, Prague.
The same happening was reported by all these stations, but
in ~~this~~ {one} tiny fraction of a millimetre, in ~~this~~ one single
second it was altered, {already} presented in conformity with the
appropriate line of propaganda, but never in accordance
with the truth. {always} Lying does not merely make use of modern
technique; it has already become a technique, a science
{whose purpose is to falsify} which is only called propaganda out of considerations of
prudence, and whose sole aim is to write history in ad-
vance, for the generations to come. Before an event has
become history - while it is still warm and alive, as one
might say - it is dissected and re-assembled. Scarcely
has an act been put into words before it becomes an official
lie. {can we take to save history from all this misrepresentations}

What counter-measures is it possible for us to take
who believe that this hypertrophy of nationalism and the
State idea in history and life is the supreme misfortune
both of our generation and the generation now growing into
manhood? How can we help to free the world from this
hypnosis? How can we ensure that history shall be taught,
not with the aim of glorifying individual nations but in
the service of the common glory of mankind? I believe

10.

that we must begin by changing our mental attitude. We
must strain every nerve to prevent ourselves from regarding
our state, our nation, our race as the focal point of the
world, simply because it happens to be ours. We must, on
the contrary, forcibly educate ourselves in the direction
of absolute objectivity. {and I maintain} To-day, such objectivity is
possible - for the first time, perhaps. Let us be just.
Let us be careful not to indict the history of yesterday
without cause. If in former times history was always
written as national history, this was excusable - indeed,
it may even have been inevitable. Let us think back to
those times. The world was not yet linked up; it was
as yet utterly impossible for the individual to view or
depict the events of our globe as a ~~composite~~ {composite} whole. How
did these old chroniclers write history? Some small monk
~~or other~~ sat in his cell ~~writing down~~ {recording} whatever occurred
within his narrow range of vision; but for him, of course,
the war which was threatening his country was the only
happening of significance. What was passing beyond the
frontiers, across the river or on the other side of the
{know only be hearsay}
mountains, he ~~did not know, and it was~~, after all, of no
importance. For an event which took place in England
had no relation to any in Germany or Italy. Even the
most convulsive upheavals - war, revolution, famine, and
pestilence - did not cast a wave from one country to an-
other. Thus it was impossible in bygone times to write
{perhaps}

11.

universal history, the history of mankind; it was only
possible to write the history of one's own nation and its
neighbours. {Willy nilly those old chroniclers} Whether they liked it or not, they had to
live and think in restricted groups. Since that time,
however, a phenomenon has emerged of which our forefathers
dared not even dream: the phenomenon of simultaneity.
For the past hundred years - {with} since the introduction of the
telegraph, the telephone, radio and the transmission of
pictures - the whole world has come to think, experience
and suffer simultaneously. Such a phenomenon as this
must - so much we can demand - also transform our outlook
{happens,}
on the world and on history. To-day, whatever happens to
{and at the same moment}
all of us. No catastrophe occurs at the other end of the
earth but we are affected by it; the smallest oscillation
spreads to-day with lightning rapidity and touches our
hearts. The whole world, which formerly consisted of
isolated particles, of ~~national~~ cells, each living its own
life, has in our century - and for the first time - become
one single organism. We think, we see and feel everything
simultaneously. We have, as it were, one heart and one
head. This demands, however, that our world should also
have a common memory - {that is} our common history, and our common
{that is pan-human}
memory one common conscience - a world conscience. To
help in the creation of this, seems to me our common and
most important task.

How can it be brought about? It is ~~my~~ belief that

12.

having, in the past century, altered our viewpoint, we
must now accustom ourselves to thinking in other ways.
We must carry out a systematic revaluation of all our
standards, above all historical standards. We must be
prepared to reject values which we have hitherto accepted
and to accept those we have hitherto rejected. We must ~~not be~~
~~afraid to be~~ {beings of being} drastic in this, ~~of~~ {in} demolish~~ing~~ triumphal
arches and ~~to~~ smash ~~ideals~~ {my yesterdays}. We must have no compunction
in passing many a statue in the public squares of our
homelands without removing our hats. We must make a merci-
less re-examination of all achievements, because we are
relating them to this new viewpoint, to their value, not
as it affects their own nation, but humanity as a whole.
We shall take up a sceptical attitude towards the great
heroes of war; we shall not speak of them exaltingly, but
see them from the moral viewpoint as Tolstoi sees them in
"War and Peace". {Then he writes}

"War began. That is to say, something happened which
is contrary both to human reason and to human nature.
Millions of men began to do one another so much injury -
by treachery, plunder, arson and murder - that every court
of justice in the world could have been furnished with suf-
ficient material to fill its files for centuries to come."

With him we shall be of the opinion that the victors
in all these bloody battles were not the better men or even
the cleverer men, and that in all military success it is
not genius alone which is decisive, but, on the contrary,
that genius is aided by chance and countless other factors.
Time after time, therefore, in our assessment of every event

13.
{definition of heroic achievement}
and every man, the one decisive question for us will be:
"What has this man done - not ~~only~~ {merely} for his Fatherland,
but for all lands, for humanity?" Let us not forget this:
in our three thousand years of history something must have
happened to enable the unclean animal called man to crawl
out of his cave and learn not only how to kill beasts and
other men, but also how to master the elements, to transport
himself by land and water, and in the course of the years
to multiply the strength of his hands by the invention of
machines. Something must have happened to man which urged
him to discover the art of writing, to gaze at the invisible
through a microscope, to examine the stars through a tele-
scope and calculate their orbits, to tame the lightning
and to transmit speech, thoughts and pictures across con-
tinents and oceans. The conquest of civilization, the
achievement of intellectual domination over the rest of
creation - are not these more important than the history
{of mankind}
{than all} of all the individual conquests of cities and countries?
Are they not the only achievements which inspire us with
the confidence that we are slowly - very slowly, I must
admit - overcoming the primitive impulses of our own natures,
that mankind is not standing still, but marching on towards
an invisible goal. Only those who have led the way along
this path will receive our veneration, and no others. Only
those who have made a contribution to ~~the community~~ {to the
common weal}, to the advancement of humanity will rank as
~~heroes~~ in our new history. Is such a fundamental

13.a

revaluation possible? A transference, as one might say,
from one account to the other, from debit to credit? I
am convinced of it, for I myself once experienced such a
change of attitude.

- 14 -

prevailing nationalism, we see everything only in relation to the
State, and those by whom we are ruled would like to force us to think
only for the State and in consonance with its aims and objects. Un-
consciously, and I fear consciously too, the teaching of history is as
subservient to the demands of the State as is man himself.

It is my conviction that everyone who feels this hyper-
trophy of nationalism and the State-idea to be the supreme misfortune
both of our generation and of the one now growing to manhood is help-
ing to free the world from a hypnosis, and that therefore history
must be taught in future not with a view to the glorification of
individual nations, but with a view to serving the common glory of all
mankind. We must change our viewpoint and, if we are to see the
world in a proper light, we must ascend a few steps higher to where,
as in a landscape, details are merged in the whole and our eyes rest
only upon a vast panorama. Such a change of viewpoint appears to me
not only possible, but in every sense full of prospects. I can still
remember {this} book, published many years ago, which was a revelation to
our young minds, because it taught us to look at natural history with
different eyes. This was a change of viewpoint such as the one of
which I am speaking. The book was by Prince Kropotkin, and it was
called Mutual Help among Men and Animals. Previously we had been told
in books innumerable that the fundamental law of nature was the
'struggle for existence'; that field and forest, marsh and lake, the
air and the ocean are one vast scene of frenzied strife and ruthless
massacre. The beasts of the field and the birds of the air hunt one
another no less fiercely than men ~~hunt one another~~, and almost as
{homo homini lupus}
cunningly. Everywhere the stronger falls upon the weaker. The whol
animal world is swayed by the instinct of mutual destruction. {that we}

While admitting that of the history of the past only
those achievements should stimulate our interest, which
have been done for the sake of the whole mankind perhaps
some of my audience will object, that these spiritual achie-
vements do not so powerfully ~~stimulate~~ stir the minds
of the young as the description of wars and battles, revo-
lutions and daring exploits. This objection is perfectly
justified. Indeed you are quite right - it is always
easier to pay homage to tangible successes, to violent
instincts. Wars have something fascinating in it like every
outburst of passion and we all felt in our boyhood more
enthusiasm for ~~Alcibiades~~ {Hannibal} or Alexander than for Solon,
the just judge and the wise emperor Marcus Aurelius.
Especially for young ~~man~~ {minds} justice, tolerance ~~minds~~ the
~~very~~ - I am sorry to have to confess it - the very
virtues, which we are most anxious to ~~praise~~ {promote}: justice,
tolerance, patience, pacifism, seem a little dull because
they lack visible and dramatic impulses. I know from
my own experience that it is more difficult and less
fruitful to portray the gentle humanity of an Erasmus
than the entertaining adventures of Casanova or the meteo-
ric rise of a Napoleon. But ~~should~~ must we therefore
yield to the unconscious desire of the masses for the
excitement and the sensational? No - never! Convinced
as we are of the terrible consequences of glorifying

force and success, we must remain firmly resolved, never

Our duty, our only and true task must be to substitute for
yesterdays conception a new, a better one - the heroism
of the unknown and not of the well known, the heroism of
the great scholars who pass their lonely lives, poor and for-
gotten, in their laboratories. The heroism of the statesman,

~~enough to prevent them~~ ..., the heroism of the sufferers and

but with the victims.
I am convinced, that ~~our~~ {the} ~~education~~ {mentality} depends
in the highest degree in what we have learned to love and
what we have learned to reject. {the history} That's why we ~~must~~ demand
this complete revaluation of hero-worship. We must fight

18b
indifferent god. No - as we want for our own life the crea-
tive significance of a moral sense, so we must ask it for the
whole of mankind. We ourselves are the heroes, the actors, the
poets, the creators of this grandiose drama, which is called
'Advance of mankind' Each of us, insignificant as his existence
may appear, has a line to speak or a gesture to make in it and
I for my part, feel greater pride in this anonymous acting
than in the futile glory, which only promotes the interests
of one particular group. Happily there are [19]

that there is a meaning in the eternal activity and endeavour of man-
kind; that each of us, insignificant as his existence may appear, has
been allotted a line or a gesture in the play; that humanity has a
mission! Just as a man only really lives when he feels that his life
has meaning, so we can only feel that history is significant when we
ourselves invest it with meaning - namely, as progress towards an
ever high stage of humanity.
{education Weltbürger}
I believe that our own progress depends upon the history of
the future being written as the history of the heroism of the spirit.
{Reading history} {eternal bridges / we} {common}
It must not cause man to despair of mankind, but must stir in him a
new pride in his humanity, in his mastery over the elements, his con-
quest of Nature, his wresting of their secrets from earth and sky.
There are already promising signs that such an interpretation of his-
{not merely}
tory is possible, ~~is~~ even on the ~~way~~ {march}. During the last decades we have
seen attempts to portray history ~~not merely as a calendar of battles,~~ 
~~as a bloody orbit of violence, but~~ as a series of steps which mankind
is ascending; and I consider that the world owes a deep debt of grati-
tude to your country for the fact that among you, in particular, this
type of book has met with the greatest success and the widest circula-
tion. I need only recall H.G. Wells's Outline of History, which repres-
ents the first balanced attempt to see world history as the mutual
fructification of the peoples. I would further mention the story of
your own nation, which the authors, Charles and Mary Beard, deliber-
ately called not 'The History of America', but The Rise of American
Civilisation. Then there is van Loon's History of Tolerance. And it
was a special satisfaction to me that a book like the biography of
Madame Curie should have won millions of hearts in this country, for
it represents the very type of historical interpretation which I hope
